posts.title: Buzz Aldrin Salutes the Flag, Apollo 11, 1969
posts.text:
Buzz Aldrin saluting the American flag on the Moon. July 20, 1969. Neil Armstrong took this photo. You can see Armstrong reflected in Aldrin's visor.

Aldrin's spacesuit weighed 180 pounds on Earth and 30 pounds on the Moon. The flag has a horizontal rod along the top because there's no wind to make it fly. They almost couldn't get the pole to stay in the ground — the lunar soil was harder than expected beneath the first few inches.

Aldrin and Armstrong spent 2 hours and 31 minutes outside the lunar module. They collected 47.5 pounds of moon rocks.

NASA, AS11-40-5874. July 20, 1969.

posts.title: Earthrise, Apollo 8, 1968
posts.text:
Earthrise. December 24, 1968. Apollo 8. William Anders took this photograph while orbiting the Moon on Christmas Eve.

Anders, Frank Borman, and Jim Lovell were the first humans to leave Earth orbit. They went around the Moon ten times. On the fourth orbit, the spacecraft rotated and Anders saw the Earth coming up over the lunar horizon.

He grabbed a roll of color film — the flight plan only called for black and white. The whole sequence took about 30 seconds. He shot four frames.

Wilderness photographer Galen Rowell called it the most influential environmental photograph ever taken.

NASA, AS08-14-2383. December 24, 1968.

tweets.text:
Earthrise. December 24, 1968. William Anders took this on Apollo 8 while orbiting the Moon on Christmas Eve.

Anders, Borman, and Lovell were the first humans to leave Earth orbit. On the fourth pass, the spacecraft rotated and Anders saw Earth rising over the lunar horizon.

He grabbed color film — the flight plan only called for B&W. The whole sequence took 30 seconds. Four frames.

Galen Rowell called it the most influential environmental photograph ever taken.

NASA, AS08-14-2383.

posts.title: The Blue Marble, Apollo 17, 1972
posts.text:
The Blue Marble. December 7, 1972. Taken by the crew of Apollo 17 at about 18,000 miles from Earth — five hours into the flight.

This is the first photograph of the fully illuminated Earth taken by a human being. You can see Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, Madagascar, and Antarctica. The Indian Ocean is cloud-free.

Apollo 17 was the last crewed mission to the Moon. Eugene Cernan, Ron Evans, and Harrison Schmitt. Nobody has been back since December 1972.

NASA, AS17-148-22727. December 7, 1972.

posts.title: Saturn V Launch, Apollo 11, 1969
posts.text:
Saturn V lifting off from Kennedy Space Center, July 16, 1969. Apollo 11. The rocket is 363 feet tall — taller than the Statue of Liberty. It weighed 6.2 million pounds fully fueled.

The five F-1 engines on the first stage produced 7.5 million pounds of thrust. Each engine burned 6,000 pounds of fuel per second. The entire first stage burned for 2 minutes and 41 seconds, then dropped into the Atlantic.

Thirteen Saturn Vs were launched. None failed. The program cost $6.4 billion in 1960s dollars — about $50 billion today.

NASA, 6900540. July 16, 1969.

posts.title: Mission Control Celebrates, Apollo 11, 1969
posts.text:
Mission Control Center, Houston. July 24, 1969. Apollo 11 has just splashed down. The flight controllers are on their feet. Cigars are lit. American flags are waving.

The man in the center with the cigar is Deke Slayton, one of the original Mercury 7 astronauts. He was grounded in 1962 for an irregular heartbeat and spent the rest of the decade picking crews for everyone else. He finally flew on Apollo-Soyuz in 1975.

The average age of the flight controllers during Apollo 11 was 26.

NASA, S69-40025. July 24, 1969.

posts.title: David Scott on Hadley Delta, Apollo 15, 1971
posts.text:
David Scott on the slope of Hadley Delta, Apollo 15. August 1, 1971. Behind him is the Lunar Roving Vehicle — the first car driven on the Moon.

The rover was built by Boeing and General Motors. It weighed 460 pounds, ran on two 36-volt batteries, and had a top speed of 8 mph. Each wheel had its own electric motor.

Scott and Jim Irwin drove 17.5 miles across the lunar surface over three days. They found the Genesis Rock — a 4-billion-year-old sample of the Moon's original crust.

The rover is still parked at Hadley-Apennine. It will be there for a very long time.

NASA, AS15-85-11437. August 1, 1971.

posts.title: Bootprint on the Moon, Apollo 11, 1969
posts.text:
Buzz Aldrin's bootprint on the lunar surface. July 20, 1969. This was a planned photograph — part of an experiment to study the properties of lunar soil.

The soil is called regolith. It's a fine powder made from billions of years of micrometeorite impacts grinding the surface rock. It behaves like wet sand but there's no moisture. The grains are jagged, not rounded by wind or water, so they lock together.

The print is about half an inch deep. There's no wind, no rain, no erosion on the Moon. This bootprint will last for millions of years.

NASA, AS11-40-5878. July 20, 1969.

tweets.text:

The soil is called regolith. Billions of years of micrometeorite impacts ground the rock to powder. Grains are jagged, not rounded by wind. They lock together like wet sand.

No wind. No rain. No erosion. This print will last millions of years.

NASA, AS11-40-5878.
